facade_colour: pale green storefront with black entry surround and white balcony/railing above

hcd_data.architectural_style: Converted House-form, Semi-detached (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that has been modified to accommodate a commercial conversion through ground floor alterations. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. With its visibly layered built form, each property is also associated with the patterns of commercial adaptation and expansion that began in the early twentieth century. Constructed as a pair of semi-detached residences, the properties have evolved over time while retaining original building features reflecting vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles, including their shared cross gabled roofline, double-height projecting bay, and segmentally arched window openings at the second storey. Each property has contextual value because it supports and maintains the historic mixed use character of the area and is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area.

facade_description: 291 Augusta Ave presents a converted house-form, semi-detached facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a gable, consistent with its converted house-form, semi-detached typology. The facade reads primarily as mixed in a pale green storefront with black entry surround and white balcony/railing above palette, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 1, 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using mixed, and 1 principal entrance. Character-defining facade elements include cornice. HCD note: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that has been modified to accommodate a commercial conversion through ground floor alterations. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. With its visibly layered built form, each property is also associated with the patterns of commercial adaptation and expansion that began in the early twentieth century. Constructed as a pair of semi-detached residences, the properties have evolved over time while retaining original building features reflecting vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles, including their shared cross gabled roofline, double-height projecting bay, and segmentally arched window openings at the second storey. Each property has contextual value because it supports and maintains the historic mixed use character of the area and is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area.
